title: Cat Jaundice: Why Your Cat's Skin is Yellow and What to Do
meta_desc: Learn why yellow skin (jaundice) in cats is a high-priority emergency and why you must seek veterinary care immediately.
content_html:
TL;DR: Jaundice is a life-threatening veterinary emergency in cats that signals serious underlying issues like liver failure or severe anemia. If your cat's skin, eyes, or gums appear yellow, seek immediate emergency veterinary care.

What is jaundice in cats and what causes the yellow discoloration?

Jaundice, also referred to as icterus, is a clinical sign where the skin, mucous membranes, and the whites of the eyes take on a distinct yellow hue. This happens when there is an accumulation of bilirubin, a yellow pigment created during the normal breakdown of red blood cells. Under normal conditions, the liver filters this pigment, but if the liver is overwhelmed, damaged, or the gallbladder is obstructed, the pigment builds up in the body's tissues.

Is jaundice or yellow skin in my cat considered a veterinary emergency?

Yes. The urgency level for jaundice in cats is high. Yellow skin is never normal and is usually a sign of a life-threatening underlying condition. Because cats can decline very rapidly once jaundice becomes visible, immediate evaluation by a veterinarian is required to check for:

  • Hepatic lipidosis (fatty liver)
  • Advanced liver disease
  • Toxin exposure
  • Severe hemolytic anemia

How does providing a photo of my cat's eyes or gums help with triage?

Providing a high-quality photograph of your cat's ears, gums, or the whites of their eyes in natural lighting is an essential tool for triage because:

  • Indoor lighting can sometimes cast a false yellow glow or hide subtle discoloration.
  • A clear photo allows a veterinary professional to confirm the presence of icterus and prioritize your cat's case effectively.
  • Visual evidence helps the medical team prepare for your arrival and understand the severity of the bilirubin buildup.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

In cats, jaundice (hyperbilirubinemia) results in yellowing of the skin and is often associated with hepatic lipidosis (HL) syndrome or primary necroinflammatory processes affecting the pancreas, liver, bile ducts, or gallbladder. Laboratory findings in jaundiced cats often reveal a nonregenerative anemia, poikilocytosis, increased red blood cell Heinz bodies, variable white blood cell count, hyperbilirubinemia, bilirubinuria, mild to marked increases in alanine transaminase (ALT) and aspartate transaminase (AST), and marked increases in alkaline phosphatase (ALP). Gamma-glutamyl transferase (GGT) activity is increased to a greater extent than ALP in cases involving the pancreas, liver, bile ducts, or gallbladder, aiding in the diagnosis of cholangitis/cholangiohepatitis and other biliary diseases, and predicting the need for liver or pancreatic biopsy. Depending on the underlying cause, hypoalbuminemia and hyperglobulinemia may also be present, and prolonged prothrombin time (PT) or activated partial thromboplastin time (APTT) may develop. Early diagnosis, full treatment support, and control of underlying disease result in a good prognosis, with a decline in total bilirubin by 50% within the first 7-10 days indicating a positive response.

Chapter: Gastroenterology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 482)

faqs.question: Where is jaundice easiest to see on a cat?
faqs.answer: It is most visible on the inside of the ear flaps, the gums, and the whites of the eyes (sclera).

faqs.question: Can a cat recover from being jaundiced?
faqs.answer: Yes, many cats can recover if the underlying cause is identified and treated early, though it often requires intensive veterinary care and hospitalization.

faqs.question: What are other symptoms often seen with yellow skin?
faqs.answer: Common concurrent symptoms include extreme lethargy, a complete loss of appetite, vomiting, and dark-colored or 'orange' looking urine.
